---
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, custom.scss]
    slide-number: true
    transition: fade
    font-family: 'Trebuchet MS'
    scrollable: true
---

<style>
body {
  font-family: 'Trebuchet MS', sans-serif;
}
.reveal h1, .reveal h2, .reveal h3, .reveal h4 {
  color: #2980b9;
}
.reveal {
  font-family: 'Trebuchet MS', sans-serif;
}
</style>

<div style="text-align:center; margin-top: 40px; line-height: 1.4;">

  <img src="https://pelprek.sgp1.digitaloceanspaces.com/banner/3489/148825024520275.png" alt="PPIU Logo" style="width: 400px; margin-bottom: 20px;">

  <h1 style="font-size: 3em; font-weight: 700; color: #2980b9; margin-bottom: 10px;">
    Programming Language I
  </h1>

  <h2 style="font-size: 1.6em; font-weight: 500; color: #555;">
    Lists, Tuples & Dictionaries
  </h2>

  <div style="border-bottom: 4px solid #e74c3c; width: 45%; margin: 18px auto;"></div>
  <h3 style="margin-top: 25px; font-weight: 700; color: #050E3C">
    By: MIN Sothearith
  </h3>

  <h4 style="font-weight: 700; color: #666;">
    Phnom Penh International University
  </h4>

</div>

## Welcome Back! 🎉

<div style="text-align:center; margin-top: 40px;">
  <h3 style="color: #2980b9;">Organizing Data in Python</h3>

  <p style="font-size: 1.4em; margin-top: 50px;">
    Today we learn how to <strong>store</strong>, <strong>organize</strong>, and <strong>access</strong> collections of data using Python's most important data structures! 📦
  </p>
</div>

::: {.callout-note}
## 💡 Why It Matters
Real programs almost never work with just one piece of data. You need to store student names, scores, product prices, settings — **collections** of related information. Lists, tuples, and dictionaries are the tools for this!
:::

## Today's Plan

**Lists, Tuples & Dictionaries — 3 Hours:**

1. **Part 1** — Lists: Creating, Accessing & Slicing
2. **Part 2** — List Methods: Adding, Removing & Sorting
3. **Part 3** — Looping Through Lists
4. **Part 4** — Tuples: Immutable Sequences
5. **Part 5** — Dictionaries: Key-Value Pairs
6. **Part 6** — Dictionary Methods & Looping
7. **Part 7** — Nested Structures & Combining Data Types
8. **Part 8** — Practice Projects & Challenges

::: {.callout-tip}
**⏱️ Time:** ~3 hours | **📝 Goal:** Master Python's core data structures for organizing information!
:::

## Learning Objectives

**By the end of this class, you will:**

- Create and manipulate **lists** (add, remove, sort, slice)
- Loop through lists using `for` loops and `enumerate()`
- Understand **tuples** and when to use them instead of lists
- Create and use **dictionaries** for key-value data
- Access, add, update, and delete dictionary entries
- Loop through dictionaries using `.keys()`, `.values()`, `.items()`
- Combine lists, tuples, and dictionaries in **nested structures**
- Build real programs using these data structures

::: {.callout-important}
## 🎯 Hands-On Learning
Every concept comes with live examples and practice exercises you will write yourself!
:::

---

# Part 1: Lists — Creating, Accessing & Slicing

## What is a List?

::: {style="text-align:center; margin-top: 30px;"}
### A **list** is an ordered, mutable collection of items enclosed in square brackets `[]` {style="color: #2980b9; font-size: 1.4em;"}
:::

```python
# Creating lists
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
scores = [85, 92, 78, 95, 88]
mixed = ["Sokha", 20, 3.75, True]   # Can mix types!
empty = []                            # Empty list

print(fruits)    # ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry']
print(scores)    # [85, 92, 78, 95, 88]
print(len(fruits))  # 3
print(len(scores))  # 5
```

::: {.callout-note}
## 💡 Key Facts About Lists
- **Ordered** — items stay in the order you put them
- **Mutable** — you CAN change items after creation (unlike strings!)
- **Allow duplicates** — `[1, 2, 2, 3]` is perfectly valid
- **Can mix types** — but usually you keep the same type
:::

## Accessing List Items (Indexing)

Just like strings, each item has a **position** (index) starting from `0`:

```python
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry", "durian", "elderberry"]
#          0         1         2         3          4
#         -5        -4        -3        -2         -1

print(fruits[0])     # "apple"     (first item)
print(fruits[2])     # "cherry"    (third item)
print(fruits[-1])    # "elderberry" (last item)
print(fruits[-2])    # "durian"    (second from last)
```

```python
# ❌ What happens if you go out of range?
print(fruits[10])    # IndexError: list index out of range
```

::: {.callout-important}
## 🎯 Same Rules as Strings!
Index starts at **0**. Last item is `list[-1]`. Going out of range causes an `IndexError`.
:::

## List Slicing

```python
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry", "durian", "elderberry"]

# Slicing syntax: list[start:stop]
print(fruits[1:4])    # ['banana', 'cherry', 'durian']
print(fruits[:3])     # ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry']
print(fruits[2:])     # ['cherry', 'durian', 'elderberry']
print(fruits[-3:])    # ['cherry', 'durian', 'elderberry']

# With step
print(fruits[::2])    # ['apple', 'cherry', 'elderberry']
print(fruits[::-1])   # ['elderberry', 'durian', 'cherry', 'banana', 'apple']
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## 💡 Slicing Rule
Same as strings! `list[start:stop]` gives items from `start` up to but **NOT including** `stop`.
:::

## Lists are Mutable! (Unlike Strings)

::: {style="text-align:center; margin-top: 30px;"}
### **Mutable** means you **CAN change** items after creating the list {style="color: #27ae60; font-size: 1.4em;"}
:::

```python
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]

# ✅ Change an item directly
fruits[1] = "blueberry"
print(fruits)    # ['apple', 'blueberry', 'cherry']

# ✅ Change a slice
fruits[0:2] = ["avocado", "blackberry"]
print(fruits)    # ['avocado', 'blackberry', 'cherry']
```

::: {.callout-warning}
## ⚠️ Remember the Difference!
**Strings** are immutable → `name[0] = "J"` gives an ERROR

**Lists** are mutable → `fruits[0] = "avocado"` works perfectly!
:::

## Practice 1A — List Basics

```python
# Create a list of 5 student names
students = ["Sokha", "Dara", "Chenda", "Bopha", "Vanna"]

# Print the list and its length
print(f"Students: {students}")
print(f"Total: {len(students)} students")

# Access specific students
print(f"First student: {students[0]}")
print(f"Last student: {students[-1]}")
print(f"Middle three: {students[1:4]}")

# Change a student name
students[2] = "Chanthy"
print(f"Updated list: {students}")
```

## Practice 1B — Score Analysis

```python
scores = [85, 92, 78, 95, 88, 72, 90, 68, 84, 96]

print(f"All scores:    {scores}")
print(f"Total scores:  {len(scores)}")
print(f"First score:   {scores[0]}")
print(f"Last score:    {scores[-1]}")
print(f"Top 3 scores:  {scores[-3:]}")
print(f"Reversed:      {scores[::-1]}")

# Check if a score exists
if 95 in scores:
    print("95 is in the list!")
    print(f"Position: index {scores.index(95)}")
```

---

# Part 2: List Methods — Adding, Removing & Sorting

## Adding Items to a List

```python
fruits = ["apple", "banana"]

# append() — add ONE item to the END
fruits.append("cherry")
print(fruits)    # ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry']

# insert() — add ONE item at a SPECIFIC position
fruits.insert(1, "avocado")
print(fruits)    # ['apple', 'avocado', 'banana', 'cherry']

# extend() — add MULTIPLE items to the END
fruits.extend(["durian", "elderberry"])
print(fruits)    # ['apple', 'avocado', 'banana', 'cherry', 'durian', 'elderberry']
```

::: {.callout-important}
## 🎯 append() vs extend()
`append()` adds **one item** (even if it's a list — it adds the whole list as one item!)

`extend()` adds **each item** from another list individually.
:::

## append() vs extend() — Be Careful!

```python
list1 = [1, 2, 3]
list2 = [1, 2, 3]

# append a list → adds the LIST as one item
list1.append([4, 5])
print(list1)    # [1, 2, 3, [4, 5]]  ← nested list!

# extend with a list → adds EACH item
list2.extend([4, 5])
print(list2)    # [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]    ← flat list!
```

```python
# You can also use + to combine lists (creates a NEW list)
a = [1, 2, 3]
b = [4, 5, 6]
c = a + b
print(c)    # [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]
print(a)    # [1, 2, 3]  ← original unchanged!
```

## Removing Items from a List

```python
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry", "banana", "durian"]

# remove() — remove the FIRST occurrence of a value
fruits.remove("banana")
print(fruits)    # ['apple', 'cherry', 'banana', 'durian']

# pop() — remove and RETURN item at a position (default: last)
last = fruits.pop()
print(last)      # "durian"
print(fruits)    # ['apple', 'cherry', 'banana']

second = fruits.pop(1)
print(second)    # "cherry"
print(fruits)    # ['apple', 'banana']

# del — delete by index
del fruits[0]
print(fruits)    # ['banana']

# clear() — remove ALL items
fruits.clear()
print(fruits)    # []
```

::: {.callout-warning}
## ⚠️ Common Error
`remove()` raises a **ValueError** if the item is not in the list! Check first with `if item in list:`.
:::

## Sorting Lists

```python
numbers = [42, 15, 88, 3, 67, 23]

# sort() — sorts IN PLACE (changes the original list)
numbers.sort()
print(numbers)    # [3, 15, 23, 42, 67, 88]

# Sort in reverse order
numbers.sort(reverse=True)
print(numbers)    # [88, 67, 42, 23, 15, 3]

# sorted() — returns a NEW sorted list (original unchanged)
original = [42, 15, 88, 3, 67]
new_sorted = sorted(original)
print(original)     # [42, 15, 88, 3, 67]  ← unchanged!
print(new_sorted)   # [3, 15, 42, 67, 88]
```

```python
# Sort strings alphabetically
names = ["Chenda", "Sokha", "Ary", "Bopha", "Dara"]
names.sort()
print(names)    # ['Ary', 'Bopha', 'Chenda', 'Dara', 'Sokha']

# reverse() — reverses the order (NOT sorting!)
names.reverse()
print(names)    # ['Sokha', 'Dara', 'Chenda', 'Bopha', 'Ary']
```

## Other Useful List Operations

```python
numbers = [10, 20, 30, 20, 40, 20]

# count() — how many times does a value appear?
print(numbers.count(20))     # 3

# index() — what position is the FIRST occurrence?
print(numbers.index(30))     # 2

# min(), max(), sum() — built-in functions
print(min(numbers))    # 10
print(max(numbers))    # 40
print(sum(numbers))    # 140

# Average
average = sum(numbers) / len(numbers)
print(f"Average: {average:.1f}")   # Average: 23.3

# copy() — make a copy of the list
backup = numbers.copy()
backup[0] = 999
print(numbers)    # [10, 20, 30, 20, 40, 20]  ← original safe!
print(backup)     # [999, 20, 30, 20, 40, 20]
```

## Summary Table — List Methods

| Method | What It Does | Example |
|--------|-------------|---------|
| `append(x)` | Add `x` to end | `[1,2].append(3)` → `[1,2,3]` |
| `insert(i, x)` | Add `x` at position `i` | `[1,3].insert(1, 2)` → `[1,2,3]` |
| `extend(list)` | Add all items from another list | `[1].extend([2,3])` → `[1,2,3]` |
| `remove(x)` | Remove first `x` | `[1,2,2].remove(2)` → `[1,2]` |
| `pop(i)` | Remove & return item at `i` | `[1,2,3].pop(1)` → returns `2` |
| `clear()` | Remove all items | `[1,2,3].clear()` → `[]` |
| `sort()` | Sort in place | `[3,1,2].sort()` → `[1,2,3]` |
| `reverse()` | Reverse in place | `[1,2,3].reverse()` → `[3,2,1]` |
| `count(x)` | Count occurrences of `x` | `[1,2,2].count(2)` → `2` |
| `index(x)` | Find position of first `x` | `[10,20,30].index(20)` → `1` |
| `copy()` | Make a shallow copy | `backup = nums.copy()` |

## Practice 2A — Shopping List Manager

```python
shopping = []

# Build the list
shopping.append("Rice")
shopping.append("Eggs")
shopping.append("Milk")
shopping.append("Bread")
shopping.append("Fish")

print("=== Shopping List ===")
for i, item in enumerate(shopping, 1):
    print(f"  {i}. {item}")

# Oops, forgot something! Insert at position 2
shopping.insert(2, "Vegetables")

# Don't need bread anymore
shopping.remove("Bread")

# Sort alphabetically
shopping.sort()

print("\n=== Updated List ===")
for i, item in enumerate(shopping, 1):
    print(f"  {i}. {item}")

print(f"\nTotal items: {len(shopping)}")
```

## Practice 2B — Grade Calculator

```python
print("=== Grade Calculator ===")
print("Enter scores (type 'done' to finish):\n")

scores = []
while True:
    entry = input("Score: ").strip()
    if entry.lower() == "done":
        break
    if entry.isdigit():
        scores.append(int(entry))
    else:
        print("  ❌ Please enter a number or 'done'")

if len(scores) > 0:
    scores.sort(reverse=True)

    print(f"\nAll scores:  {scores}")
    print(f"Highest:     {max(scores)}")
    print(f"Lowest:      {min(scores)}")
    print(f"Average:     {sum(scores) / len(scores):.1f}")
    print(f"Total:       {sum(scores)}")

    # Count grades
    a_count = sum(1 for s in scores if s >= 90)
    b_count = sum(1 for s in scores if 80 <= s < 90)
    c_count = sum(1 for s in scores if 70 <= s < 80)
    f_count = sum(1 for s in scores if s < 70)

    print(f"\nGrade A (90+): {a_count}")
    print(f"Grade B (80-89): {b_count}")
    print(f"Grade C (70-79): {c_count}")
    print(f"Grade F (<70): {f_count}")
else:
    print("No scores entered.")
```

---

# Part 3: Looping Through Lists

## `for` Loop with Lists

```python
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry", "durian"]

# Simple loop — get each item
for fruit in fruits:
    print(f"I like {fruit}!")

# Output:
# I like apple!
# I like banana!
# I like cherry!
# I like durian!
```

```python
# Loop with numbers
scores = [85, 92, 78, 95, 88]

total = 0
for score in scores:
    total += score

average = total / len(scores)
print(f"Average: {average:.1f}")    # Average: 87.6
```

## `enumerate()` — Get Index AND Value

```python
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry", "durian"]

# Without enumerate — just values
for fruit in fruits:
    print(fruit)

# With enumerate — index AND value
for index, fruit in enumerate(fruits):
    print(f"{index}: {fruit}")
# 0: apple
# 1: banana
# 2: cherry
# 3: durian

# Start counting from 1 (for display)
for num, fruit in enumerate(fruits, 1):
    print(f"{num}. {fruit}")
# 1. apple
# 2. banana
# 3. cherry
# 4. durian
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## 💡 When to Use `enumerate()`
Use it whenever you need both the **position** and the **value**. It's cleaner than using `range(len(list))`.
:::

## `range()` with Lists

```python
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry", "durian"]

# Using range(len()) — less Pythonic but sometimes useful
for i in range(len(fruits)):
    print(f"Index {i}: {fruits[i]}")

# Useful when you need to MODIFY items
scores = [85, 92, 78, 95, 88]

# Add 5 bonus points to every score
for i in range(len(scores)):
    scores[i] += 5

print(scores)    # [90, 97, 83, 100, 93]
```

## Building Lists with Loops

```python
# Create a list of squares
squares = []
for i in range(1, 11):
    squares.append(i ** 2)
print(squares)    # [1, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36, 49, 64, 81, 100]

# Filter: keep only passing scores
all_scores = [85, 42, 92, 55, 78, 38, 95, 61]
passing = []
for score in all_scores:
    if score >= 60:
        passing.append(score)
print(f"Passing: {passing}")    # [85, 92, 78, 95, 61]
```

```python
# List comprehension — a shortcut! (same result)
squares = [i ** 2 for i in range(1, 11)]
print(squares)    # [1, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36, 49, 64, 81, 100]

passing = [s for s in all_scores if s >= 60]
print(passing)    # [85, 92, 78, 95, 61]
```

::: {.callout-note}
## 💡 List Comprehension
`[expression for item in list]` — a compact way to create lists.

`[expression for item in list if condition]` — with filtering.

It does the same thing as a loop + append, just shorter!
:::

## Practice 3A — Student Roster

```python
students = ["sokha", "dara", "CHENDA", "bopha", " Vanna "]

# Clean up names
clean_students = []
for name in students:
    clean_students.append(name.strip().title())

print("=== Student Roster ===")
for i, name in enumerate(clean_students, 1):
    print(f"  {i}. {name}")

# Find longest name
longest = max(clean_students, key=len)
print(f"\nLongest name: {longest} ({len(longest)} letters)")
```

---

# Part 4: Tuples — Immutable Sequences

## What is a Tuple?

::: {style="text-align:center; margin-top: 30px;"}
### A **tuple** is an ordered, **immutable** collection enclosed in parentheses `()` {style="color: #2980b9; font-size: 1.4em;"}
:::

```python
# Creating tuples
coordinates = (10, 20)
colors = ("red", "green", "blue")
person = ("Sokha", 20, "Computer Science")
single = (42,)     # ← comma is required for single-item tuple!
empty = ()

print(coordinates)    # (10, 20)
print(colors)         # ('red', 'green', 'blue')
print(len(colors))    # 3
```

::: {.callout-warning}
## ⚠️ Single-Item Tuple Trap
`(42)` is just the number 42 in parentheses!

`(42,)` is a tuple with one item — the **comma** makes it a tuple!
:::

## Accessing Tuple Items

```python
colors = ("red", "green", "blue", "yellow", "purple")

# Indexing — same as lists
print(colors[0])      # "red"
print(colors[-1])     # "purple"
print(colors[1:3])    # ('green', 'blue')

# ✅ You CAN read items
print(colors[2])      # "blue"

# ❌ You CANNOT change items
colors[0] = "pink"    # TypeError: 'tuple' object does not support item assignment
```

## Tuples are Immutable — Why Use Them?

```python
# Tuple unpacking — assign multiple values at once!
person = ("Sokha", 20, "Computer Science")

name, age, major = person    # Unpack into 3 variables
print(name)     # "Sokha"
print(age)      # 20
print(major)    # "Computer Science"
```

```python
# Swap two variables using tuple unpacking
a = 10
b = 20
a, b = b, a    # Swap!
print(a, b)    # 20 10
```

**When to use tuples instead of lists:**

- Data that should **NOT change** (coordinates, RGB colors, dates)
- **Returning multiple values** from a function
- **Dictionary keys** (lists cannot be keys, tuples can!)
- When you want to signal "this data is fixed"

::: {.callout-tip}
## 💡 Simple Rule
If data should change → use a **list**

If data should stay fixed → use a **tuple**
:::

## Tuple Methods & Operations

```python
colors = ("red", "green", "blue", "green", "red", "red")

# count() — how many times?
print(colors.count("red"))     # 3

# index() — first position?
print(colors.index("green"))   # 1

# Membership test
print("blue" in colors)        # True

# Length
print(len(colors))             # 6

# Convert between list and tuple
my_list = list(colors)         # Tuple → List (now mutable!)
my_tuple = tuple(my_list)      # List → Tuple (now immutable!)
```

## Practice 4A — Student Records with Tuples

```python
# Each student is a tuple: (name, score, grade)
students = [
    ("Sokha", 92, "A"),
    ("Dara", 78, "C"),
    ("Chenda", 85, "B"),
    ("Bopha", 95, "A"),
    ("Vanna", 68, "D"),
]

print("=== Student Report ===")
print(f"{'Name':<12} {'Score':>6} {'Grade':>6}")
print("-" * 26)

for name, score, grade in students:
    print(f"{name:<12} {score:>6} {grade:>6}")

# Find top student
top_student = max(students, key=lambda s: s[1])
print(f"\nTop student: {top_student[0]} with {top_student[1]} points!")

# Calculate average
avg = sum(s[1] for s in students) / len(students)
print(f"Class average: {avg:.1f}")
```

---

# Part 5: Dictionaries — Key-Value Pairs

## What is a Dictionary?

::: {style="text-align:center; margin-top: 30px;"}
### A **dictionary** stores data as **key: value** pairs inside curly braces `{}` {style="color: #2980b9; font-size: 1.4em;"}
:::

```python
# Creating a dictionary
student = {
    "name": "Sokha",
    "age": 20,
    "major": "Computer Science",
    "gpa": 3.75
}

print(student)
# {'name': 'Sokha', 'age': 20, 'major': 'Computer Science', 'gpa': 3.75}
```

```python
# Empty dictionary
empty = {}

# Dictionary with different value types
profile = {
    "username": "sokha_dev",
    "followers": 150,
    "is_active": True,
    "skills": ["Python", "HTML", "CSS"]   # Value can be a list!
}
```

::: {.callout-note}
## 💡 Key Facts About Dictionaries
- **Unordered** (Python 3.7+ keeps insertion order, but don't rely on it for logic)
- **Mutable** — you can add, change, and delete entries
- **Keys must be unique** — no duplicate keys allowed
- **Keys must be immutable** — strings, numbers, tuples can be keys (NOT lists!)
- **Values can be anything** — strings, numbers, lists, other dicts...
:::

## Accessing Dictionary Values

```python
student = {
    "name": "Sokha",
    "age": 20,
    "major": "Computer Science",
    "gpa": 3.75
}

# Method 1: Square brackets (raises KeyError if key doesn't exist)
print(student["name"])     # "Sokha"
print(student["gpa"])      # 3.75
# print(student["email"])  # ❌ KeyError: 'email'

# Method 2: .get() (returns None or default if key doesn't exist)
print(student.get("name"))         # "Sokha"
print(student.get("email"))        # None  (no error!)
print(student.get("email", "N/A")) # "N/A" (custom default)
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## 💡 Use `.get()` to Be Safe
`dict["key"]` crashes if the key is missing. `dict.get("key")` returns `None` safely. Use `.get()` when you're not sure if the key exists!
:::

## Adding & Updating Dictionary Entries

```python
student = {"name": "Sokha", "age": 20}

# Add a new key-value pair
student["major"] = "Computer Science"
student["gpa"] = 3.75
print(student)
# {'name': 'Sokha', 'age': 20, 'major': 'Computer Science', 'gpa': 3.75}

# Update an existing value
student["age"] = 21
print(student["age"])    # 21

# Update multiple values at once
student.update({"gpa": 3.80, "year": 3})
print(student)
# {'name': 'Sokha', 'age': 21, 'major': 'Computer Science', 'gpa': 3.8, 'year': 3}
```

## Removing Dictionary Entries

```python
student = {
    "name": "Sokha",
    "age": 20,
    "major": "CS",
    "gpa": 3.75,
    "temp": "delete me"
}

# pop() — remove and return a value
removed = student.pop("temp")
print(removed)       # "delete me"
print(student)       # temp is gone

# pop() with default (no error if key missing)
result = student.pop("email", "Not found")
print(result)        # "Not found"

# del — delete by key
del student["gpa"]
print(student)       # gpa is gone

# clear() — remove everything
# student.clear()    # {}
```

## Checking if a Key Exists

```python
student = {"name": "Sokha", "age": 20, "major": "CS"}

# Check with 'in' (checks KEYS, not values)
print("name" in student)       # True
print("email" in student)      # False
print("Sokha" in student)      # False  (checks keys, not values!)

# Safe pattern: check before accessing
if "email" in student:
    print(student["email"])
else:
    print("No email on file")

# Or use .get()
email = student.get("email", "No email on file")
print(email)    # "No email on file"
```

## Practice 5A — Student Profile Builder

```python
print("=== Student Profile Builder ===\n")

# Collect info
profile = {}
profile["name"] = input("Name: ").strip().title()
profile["id"] = input("Student ID: ").strip().upper()
profile["major"] = input("Major: ").strip().title()
profile["year"] = input("Year (1-4): ").strip()

# Add GPA
gpa_input = input("GPA (or press Enter to skip): ").strip()
if gpa_input:
    profile["gpa"] = float(gpa_input)

# Display
print("\n" + "=" * 35)
print(f"{'STUDENT PROFILE':^35}")
print("=" * 35)
for key, value in profile.items():
    print(f"  {key.upper():<10}: {value}")
print("=" * 35)
```

---

# Part 6: Dictionary Methods & Looping

## Looping Through Dictionaries

```python
student = {"name": "Sokha", "age": 20, "major": "CS", "gpa": 3.75}

# Loop through KEYS (default)
for key in student:
    print(key)
# name, age, major, gpa

# Loop through VALUES
for value in student.values():
    print(value)
# Sokha, 20, CS, 3.75

# Loop through KEY-VALUE pairs
for key, value in student.items():
    print(f"{key}: {value}")
# name: Sokha
# age: 20
# major: CS
# gpa: 3.75
```

::: {.callout-important}
## 🎯 The Three Ways to Loop
- `for key in dict:` or `for key in dict.keys():` → get **keys**
- `for value in dict.values():` → get **values**
- `for key, value in dict.items():` → get **both** (most common!)
:::

## Dictionary `.keys()`, `.values()`, `.items()`

```python
student = {"name": "Sokha", "age": 20, "major": "CS"}

# Get all keys as a list
keys = list(student.keys())
print(keys)      # ['name', 'age', 'major']

# Get all values as a list
values = list(student.values())
print(values)    # ['Sokha', 20, 'CS']

# Get all pairs as a list of tuples
items = list(student.items())
print(items)     # [('name', 'Sokha'), ('age', 20), ('major', 'CS')]
```

## Useful Dictionary Patterns

```python
# Pattern 1: Counting occurrences
sentence = "the cat sat on the mat the cat"
word_count = {}
for word in sentence.split():
    if word in word_count:
        word_count[word] += 1
    else:
        word_count[word] = 1
print(word_count)
# {'the': 3, 'cat': 2, 'sat': 1, 'on': 1, 'mat': 1}
```

```python
# Pattern 2: Counting with .get() (shorter!)
word_count = {}
for word in sentence.split():
    word_count[word] = word_count.get(word, 0) + 1
print(word_count)
# Same result: {'the': 3, 'cat': 2, 'sat': 1, 'on': 1, 'mat': 1}
```

```python
# Pattern 3: Grouping items
scores = [("Sokha", 92), ("Dara", 78), ("Sokha", 88), ("Dara", 85)]
grouped = {}
for name, score in scores:
    if name not in grouped:
        grouped[name] = []
    grouped[name].append(score)
print(grouped)    # {'Sokha': [92, 88], 'Dara': [78, 85]}
```

## Practice 6A — Word Frequency Counter

```python
text = input("Enter a sentence: ").lower()
words = text.split()

# Count each word
frequency = {}
for word in words:
    frequency[word] = frequency.get(word, 0) + 1

# Display sorted by frequency (highest first)
print(f"\n{'Word':<15} {'Count':>5}")
print("-" * 22)

sorted_words = sorted(frequency.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)
for word, count in sorted_words:
    bar = "█" * count
    print(f"{word:<15} {count:>5}  {bar}")

print(f"\nUnique words: {len(frequency)}")
print(f"Total words:  {len(words)}")
```

## Practice 6B — Contact Book

```python
contacts = {}

print("=== Contact Book ===")
print("Commands: add, search, list, delete, quit\n")

while True:
    cmd = input(">> ").strip().lower()

    if cmd == "add":
        name = input("  Name: ").strip().title()
        phone = input("  Phone: ").strip()
        contacts[name] = phone
        print(f"  ✅ Added {name}!")

    elif cmd == "search":
        name = input("  Search name: ").strip().title()
        phone = contacts.get(name)
        if phone:
            print(f"  📞 {name}: {phone}")
        else:
            print(f"  ❌ '{name}' not found.")

    elif cmd == "list":
        if contacts:
            print(f"\n  {'Name':<15} {'Phone':>12}")
            print("  " + "-" * 28)
            for name, phone in sorted(contacts.items()):
                print(f"  {name:<15} {phone:>12}")
            print(f"\n  Total: {len(contacts)} contacts")
        else:
            print("  📭 No contacts yet.")

    elif cmd == "delete":
        name = input("  Delete name: ").strip().title()
        if name in contacts:
            del contacts[name]
            print(f"  🗑️ Deleted {name}.")
        else:
            print(f"  ❌ '{name}' not found.")

    elif cmd == "quit":
        print("  Goodbye! 👋")
        break

    else:
        print("  ❓ Unknown command.")
```

---

# Part 7: Nested Structures & Combining Data Types

## Lists of Dictionaries

::: {style="text-align:center; margin-top: 30px;"}
### The most common pattern: a **list** of **dictionaries** {style="color: #2980b9; font-size: 1.4em;"}
:::

```python
students = [
    {"name": "Sokha", "age": 20, "gpa": 3.75},
    {"name": "Dara", "age": 21, "gpa": 3.50},
    {"name": "Chenda", "age": 19, "gpa": 3.90},
    {"name": "Bopha", "age": 22, "gpa": 3.20},
]

# Access a specific student
print(students[0]["name"])     # "Sokha"
print(students[2]["gpa"])      # 3.9

# Loop through all students
for student in students:
    print(f"{student['name']} — GPA: {student['gpa']}")
```

::: {.callout-note}
## 💡 Think of it Like a Table!
Each dictionary is a **row**, each key is a **column**. This is how real databases store data!
:::

## Dictionary with List Values

```python
# Each key maps to a list of values
grades = {
    "Sokha": [85, 92, 88, 95],
    "Dara": [78, 82, 75, 80],
    "Chenda": [92, 95, 98, 90],
}

# Access Sokha's grades
print(grades["Sokha"])         # [85, 92, 88, 95]
print(grades["Sokha"][0])      # 85  (first grade)

# Calculate each student's average
for name, score_list in grades.items():
    avg = sum(score_list) / len(score_list)
    print(f"{name}: avg = {avg:.1f}")
# Sokha: avg = 90.0
# Dara: avg = 78.8
# Chenda: avg = 93.8

# Add a new grade for Sokha
grades["Sokha"].append(90)
print(grades["Sokha"])    # [85, 92, 88, 95, 90]
```

## Nested Dictionaries

```python
# Dictionary inside a dictionary
school = {
    "CS101": {
        "title": "Intro to Programming",
        "instructor": "MIN Sothearith",
        "students": 35,
        "room": "A201"
    },
    "CS201": {
        "title": "Data Structures",
        "instructor": "Dr. Sok",
        "students": 28,
        "room": "B105"
    }
}

# Access nested values
print(school["CS101"]["title"])        # "Intro to Programming"
print(school["CS201"]["instructor"])   # "Dr. Sok"

# Loop through courses
for code, info in school.items():
    print(f"{code}: {info['title']} ({info['students']} students)")
```

## Practice 7A — Class Gradebook

```python
gradebook = [
    {"name": "Sokha", "scores": [85, 92, 88]},
    {"name": "Dara", "scores": [78, 65, 72]},
    {"name": "Chenda", "scores": [95, 98, 92]},
    {"name": "Bopha", "scores": [88, 82, 90]},
    {"name": "Vanna", "scores": [60, 55, 68]},
]

print("=" * 50)
print(f"{'CLASS GRADEBOOK':^50}")
print("=" * 50)
print(f"{'Name':<12} {'Scores':<20} {'Avg':>6} {'Grade':>6}")
print("-" * 50)

for student in gradebook:
    name = student["name"]
    scores = student["scores"]
    avg = sum(scores) / len(scores)

    # Determine grade
    if avg >= 90:
        grade = "A"
    elif avg >= 80:
        grade = "B"
    elif avg >= 70:
        grade = "C"
    elif avg >= 60:
        grade = "D"
    else:
        grade = "F"

    scores_str = ", ".join(str(s) for s in scores)
    print(f"{name:<12} {scores_str:<20} {avg:>6.1f} {grade:>6}")

# Class statistics
all_avgs = [sum(s["scores"]) / len(s["scores"]) for s in gradebook]
print("-" * 50)
print(f"{'Class Average:':<32} {sum(all_avgs)/len(all_avgs):>6.1f}")
print(f"{'Highest Average:':<32} {max(all_avgs):>6.1f}")
print(f"{'Lowest Average:':<32} {min(all_avgs):>6.1f}")
```

---

# Part 8: Full Practice Projects

## Project 1 — Student Management System

```python
print("=" * 45)
print("     🎓 Student Management System")
print("=" * 45)

students = []

while True:
    print("\n[1] Add Student  [2] View All  [3] Search")
    print("[4] Statistics   [5] Quit")
    choice = input("\nChoice: ").strip()

    if choice == "1":
        name = input("  Name: ").strip().title()
        sid = input("  ID: ").strip().upper()
        score = input("  Score: ").strip()

        if not score.isdigit():
            print("  ❌ Invalid score!")
            continue

        students.append({
            "name": name,
            "id": sid,
            "score": int(score)
        })
        print(f"  ✅ Added {name}!")

    elif choice == "2":
        if not students:
            print("  📭 No students yet.")
            continue

        print(f"\n  {'Name':<15} {'ID':<10} {'Score':>6}")
        print("  " + "-" * 33)
        for s in students:
            print(f"  {s['name']:<15} {s['id']:<10} {s['score']:>6}")

    elif choice == "3":
        query = input("  Search name: ").strip().lower()
        found = [s for s in students if query in s["name"].lower()]

        if found:
            for s in found:
                print(f"  📋 {s['name']} (ID: {s['id']}) — Score: {s['score']}")
        else:
            print("  ❌ No match found.")

    elif choice == "4":
        if not students:
            print("  📭 No data.")
            continue

        scores = [s["score"] for s in students]
        print(f"\n  Total students: {len(students)}")
        print(f"  Average score:  {sum(scores)/len(scores):.1f}")
        print(f"  Highest score:  {max(scores)}")
        print(f"  Lowest score:   {min(scores)}")

    elif choice == "5":
        print("  Goodbye! 👋")
        break

    else:
        print("  ❓ Invalid choice.")
```

## Project 2 — Inventory Tracker

```python
print("=" * 50)
print("     📦 Simple Inventory Tracker")
print("=" * 50)

inventory = {}

while True:
    print("\n[1] Add Item  [2] Update Stock  [3] View All")
    print("[4] Search    [5] Low Stock Alert  [6] Quit")
    choice = input("\nChoice: ").strip()

    if choice == "1":
        name = input("  Item name: ").strip().title()
        qty = input("  Quantity: ").strip()
        price = input("  Price ($): ").strip()

        if not qty.isdigit():
            print("  ❌ Invalid quantity!")
            continue

        inventory[name] = {
            "qty": int(qty),
            "price": float(price)
        }
        print(f"  ✅ Added {name}!")

    elif choice == "2":
        name = input("  Item name: ").strip().title()
        if name not in inventory:
            print(f"  ❌ '{name}' not found.")
            continue

        change = input("  Add/remove how many? (+5 or -3): ").strip()
        try:
            inventory[name]["qty"] += int(change)
            if inventory[name]["qty"] < 0:
                inventory[name]["qty"] = 0
            print(f"  ✅ {name} stock: {inventory[name]['qty']}")
        except ValueError:
            print("  ❌ Invalid number!")

    elif choice == "3":
        if not inventory:
            print("  📭 Inventory is empty.")
            continue

        print(f"\n  {'Item':<18} {'Qty':>5} {'Price':>8} {'Value':>10}")
        print("  " + "-" * 43)
        total_value = 0
        for name, info in sorted(inventory.items()):
            value = info["qty"] * info["price"]
            total_value += value
            print(f"  {name:<18} {info['qty']:>5} ${info['price']:>7.2f} ${value:>9.2f}")
        print("  " + "-" * 43)
        print(f"  {'Total Inventory Value:':>31} ${total_value:>9.2f}")

    elif choice == "4":
        query = input("  Search: ").strip().lower()
        found = {k: v for k, v in inventory.items() if query in k.lower()}
        if found:
            for name, info in found.items():
                print(f"  📋 {name}: {info['qty']} units @ ${info['price']:.2f}")
        else:
            print("  ❌ No match.")

    elif choice == "5":
        threshold = 5
        low = {k: v for k, v in inventory.items() if v["qty"] <= threshold}
        if low:
            print(f"\n  ⚠️ Items with {threshold} or fewer units:")
            for name, info in low.items():
                print(f"    🔴 {name}: {info['qty']} left")
        else:
            print("  ✅ All items are well-stocked!")

    elif choice == "6":
        print("  Goodbye! 👋")
        break

    else:
        print("  ❓ Invalid choice.")
```

## Project 3 — Quiz Game

```python
print("=" * 40)
print("     🧠 Python Quiz Game")
print("=" * 40)

# Quiz data: list of dictionaries
quiz = [
    {
        "question": "What data type is [1, 2, 3]?",
        "options": ["A. String", "B. Tuple", "C. List", "D. Dictionary"],
        "answer": "C"
    },
    {
        "question": "Which bracket is used for dictionaries?",
        "options": ["A. []", "B. ()", "C. <>", "D. {}"],
        "answer": "D"
    },
    {
        "question": "What does append() do?",
        "options": ["A. Removes an item", "B. Adds to the end", "C. Sorts the list", "D. Reverses the list"],
        "answer": "B"
    },
    {
        "question": "Tuples are ___?",
        "options": ["A. Mutable", "B. Immutable", "C. Unordered", "D. Empty"],
        "answer": "B"
    },
    {
        "question": "How do you access dict value safely?",
        "options": ["A. dict[key]", "B. dict.find(key)", "C. dict.get(key)", "D. dict.value(key)"],
        "answer": "C"
    },
]

score = 0
total = len(quiz)

for i, q in enumerate(quiz, 1):
    print(f"\nQuestion {i}/{total}: {q['question']}")
    for option in q["options"]:
        print(f"  {option}")

    user_answer = input("\nYour answer (A/B/C/D): ").strip().upper()

    if user_answer == q["answer"]:
        print("  ✅ Correct!")
        score += 1
    else:
        print(f"  ❌ Wrong! The answer was {q['answer']}.")

# Results
print("\n" + "=" * 40)
print(f"  Score: {score}/{total} ({score/total*100:.0f}%)")
if score == total:
    print("  🏆 Perfect score! Amazing!")
elif score >= total * 0.7:
    print("  👍 Good job! Keep learning!")
else:
    print("  📚 Keep practicing! You'll get better!")
print("=" * 40)
```

---

## Comparison: List vs Tuple vs Dictionary

| Feature | List `[]` | Tuple `()` | Dictionary `{}` |
|---------|----------|------------|-----------------|
| **Ordered** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ (insertion order) |
| **Mutable** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **Duplicates** | ✅ Allowed | ✅ Allowed | ❌ Keys unique |
| **Access by** | Index `[0]` | Index `[0]` | Key `["name"]` |
| **Best for** | Collections that change | Fixed data | Key-value lookups |
| **Example** | `[85, 92, 78]` | `(10, 20)` | `{"name": "Sokha"}` |

::: {.callout-important}
## 🎯 When to Use Each

- **List** → A collection of similar items (student names, scores, items)
- **Tuple** → Fixed data that shouldn't change (coordinates, RGB colors, dates)
- **Dictionary** → Data with labels/names (student profile, settings, config)
:::

## Summary: What You've Learned

**Lists:**

- Create: `fruits = ["apple", "banana"]`
- Access: `fruits[0]`, `fruits[-1]`, `fruits[1:3]`
- Modify: `append()`, `insert()`, `remove()`, `pop()`, `sort()`
- Loop: `for item in list:`, `for i, item in enumerate(list):`

**Tuples:**

- Create: `point = (10, 20)`
- Access: `point[0]`, `point[1]`
- Unpack: `x, y = point`
- Immutable — cannot change after creation

**Dictionaries:**

- Create: `student = {"name": "Sokha", "age": 20}`
- Access: `student["name"]` or `student.get("name")`
- Modify: `student["age"] = 21`, `student.update({...})`
- Loop: `for key, value in dict.items():`

::: {.callout-tip}
## 🚀 Key Takeaways
- **Lists** are your go-to for ordered collections — use `append()` and `for` loops constantly
- **Tuples** are for fixed data — use unpacking to make code cleaner
- **Dictionaries** are for labeled data — use `.get()` for safe access
- **Nested structures** (list of dicts) are how real programs organize data
- Always use `in` to check membership before accessing: `if key in dict:`
:::

## Common Mistakes to Avoid

```python
# ❌ Mistake 1: Modifying a list while looping over it
numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
for n in numbers:
    if n % 2 == 0:
        numbers.remove(n)    # ❌ Skips items!
# ✅ Use a new list instead:
numbers = [n for n in numbers if n % 2 != 0]

# ❌ Mistake 2: Using dict["key"] without checking
student = {"name": "Sokha"}
print(student["email"])          # ❌ KeyError!
print(student.get("email", "N/A"))  # ✅ Safe!

# ❌ Mistake 3: Confusing append and extend
items = [1, 2, 3]
items.append([4, 5])     # ❌ [1, 2, 3, [4, 5]]
items.extend([4, 5])     # ✅ [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]

# ❌ Mistake 4: Forgetting the comma in single-item tuple
t = (42)     # ❌ This is just the integer 42!
t = (42,)    # ✅ This is a tuple!

# ❌ Mistake 5: Using a list as a dictionary key
d = {[1, 2]: "hello"}    # ❌ TypeError! Lists are mutable
d = {(1, 2): "hello"}    # ✅ Tuples can be keys!
```

## Quick Reference Card

```python
# --- LISTS ---
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
fruits.append("durian")        # Add to end
fruits.insert(1, "avocado")    # Add at position
fruits.extend(["fig", "grape"])# Add multiple
fruits.remove("banana")        # Remove by value
fruits.pop()                   # Remove last
fruits.sort()                  # Sort in place
fruits.reverse()               # Reverse in place
len(fruits)                    # Count items
"apple" in fruits              # Check membership

# --- TUPLES ---
point = (10, 20)
x, y = point                  # Unpack
point.count(10)                # Count value
point.index(20)                # Find position
list(point)                    # Convert to list

# --- DICTIONARIES ---
d = {"name": "Sokha", "age": 20}
d["major"] = "CS"             # Add/update
d.get("email", "N/A")         # Safe access
d.pop("age")                  # Remove key
d.update({"gpa": 3.75})       # Update multiple
d.keys()                      # All keys
d.values()                    # All values
d.items()                     # All key-value pairs
"name" in d                   # Check key exists
```

## Next Class

Next class we will dive deeper into **Functions** — writing your own reusable blocks of code!

::: {.callout-tip}
## 🚀 Get Ready
Functions + Data structures = powerful programs! Imagine writing a function that calculates the average of ANY list, or a function that searches a dictionary of students.

**Practice at home:** Write a program that:

- Stores a shopping list and calculates the total price
- Creates a phonebook using a dictionary with add/search/delete
- Takes a list of scores and returns a dictionary with statistics (min, max, avg, count)
:::
